g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Early Binding vs Late Binding in C++. This fundamental concept powers polymorphism in object-oriented programming.
g.text: Early Binding vs Late Binding in C++

g.data-narration: Binding is the process of linking a function call to its actual implementation. When this linking happens determines whether we have early or late binding.
g.text: Binding = linking a function call → to its implementation
The TIMING of this association determines the type of binding
This timing affects performance and flexibility of your code

g.data-narration: Early binding resolves at compile time - fast but inflexible. Late binding resolves at runtime - slight overhead but enables true polymorphism.
Here's an early binding example. Without the virtual keyword, the compiler uses the pointer type to determine which function to call at compile time.
g.text: Early Binding Example - No Virtual Keyword
ptr->display(); // Calls Base::display() - determined at compile time
✗ Result: Early binding calls Base method, NOT Derived!

g.data-narration: With virtual functions, late binding kicks in. The actual object type determines which function runs - the power of polymorphism!
g.text: Late Binding Example - With Virtual Keyword
ptr->display(); // Calls Derived::display() - resolved at runtime!
✓ Result: Late binding calls the correct method based on actual object type

g.data-narration: Virtual functions are the key to late binding. The virtual keyword tells the compiler to use dynamic dispatch through the vtable mechanism.
g.text: Virtual Functions - Key to Late Binding
Virtual function: A member function declared with 'virtual' that can be overridden in derived classes
The compiler creates a vtable (virtual function table) for each class with virtual functions
Each object contains a hidden vptr pointing to its class's vtable

g.data-narration: The vtable is a runtime data structure. When you call a virtual function, the program uses the vptr to look up the correct function address in the vtable.
Virtual destructors are essential! Without them, deleting a derived object through a base pointer causes memory leaks by skipping the derived destructor.
g.text: ✗ ALWAYS make base class destructor virtual when using polymorphism!

g.data-narration: Pure virtual functions (declared with = 0) make a class abstract. Abstract classes cannot be instantiated but define interfaces for derived classes.
g.text: virtual void display() = 0; // Pure virtual - must be overridden
• Declared with '= 0' - makes function pure virtual
• Derived classes MUST override all pure virtual functions

g.data-narration: RTTI (Runtime Type Information) provides dynamic_cast and typeid for safe type checking. They only work with polymorphic types having virtual functions.
g.text: RTTI features work only with polymorphic types (classes with virtual functions):

g.data-narration: There's a performance trade-off: early binding is faster with no overhead, while late binding has slight overhead from vtable lookup but enables flexibility.
Watch out for common mistakes: forgetting virtual destructors, object slicing when passing by value, and confusing function hiding with overriding.
Remember these key syntax patterns: virtual function declaration, pure virtual with = 0, virtual destructor, and override specifier from C++11.
In summary: Early binding = compile time, fast but inflexible. Late binding = runtime, enables polymorphism through virtual functions and vtables.
g.text: Remember: Is the function virtual? → That's your key to determining late binding!

g.data-narration: For exams: Always check for virtual keyword, remember vtable mechanism, and never forget virtual destructors for polymorphic deletion.

g.text: 2. Pointer/reference type matters in early binding

g.data-narration: Thank you for watching! Practice with virtual functions to master polymorphism. This is foundational for advanced C++ and object-oriented design.
g.text: ✓ Topic Complete - Practice with Virtual Functions!
university BCS306B - Object Oriented Programming with C++
